title: How Trump’s threats against Venezuela unfolded | FT #shorts

transcript.text: This is a momentous time for the
authoritarian oil richch state of
Venezuela, but also for the Trump
administration and indeed the entire
global order. There are many unanswered
questions, but what seems clear is that
America has launched an extraordinary
and audacious act of regime change in
Venezuela. Donald Trump, the US
president, has issued a brief statement
saying that Venezuela's president,
Nicolas Maduro, has been captured and
flown out of the country. This followed
a series of strikes on a military
complex, an air base, and elsewhere in
Venezuela. We should not be surprised.
For 2 months, America has been gathering
forces in the Caribbean. It's built up a
huge fleet, including an aircraft
carrier. And in this period, it's bombed
some 35 small boats, accusing them of
fing drugs. in particular cocaine to
America. It's also killed over a hundred
people. America's also been trying to
build up a case for action, arguing that
Majuro is not a legitimate head of state
and that he presides over a narco
cartel. The big question is what happens
next for Venezuela. This is a huge
moment. Majuro and his predecessor Hugo
Chavez have for over 25 years presided
over a corrupt and repressive regime.
Opposition leaders have had to go into
exile. Some six or seven million
Venezuelans have fled the borders. The
country has been bankrupted. But what
will people close to Maduro, in
particular the army, do now? Can the
opposition leaders return? And what
deal, if any, has been struck between
figures close to Maduro and the Trump
administration over what happens next?
But hovering over this is an even bigger
question. The West has tried to enforce
regime change several times in recent
decades and often with unhappy results.
Can this one really be different? And is
there a plan for what happens next?
